perturbation.original_text: 4.3 Each Party shall promptly provide to the other Party a written invention disclosure of each Agreement Invention made by its employees that results directly from the present Agreement for worked performed under the SOWs herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3 Each Party should endeavor to provide to the other Party an invention disclosure of each Agreement Invention made by its employees that seems to result from the present Agreement for worked performed under the SOWs herein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly provide' to 'should endeavor to provide' and 'results directly' to 'seems to result' weakens the obligation to disclose inventions, creating ambiguity and potentially allowing a party to avoid disclosing relevant inventions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 35 U.S. Code § 290 - Notice of patent suits
perturbation.law_explanation: While 35 U.S. Code § 290 is about notification of patent suits, the broader principle is that patent-related matters must be handled with clear and timely communication. By weakening the requirement for prompt and clear disclosure of inventions, the modified clause introduces ambiguity that hinders the ability to assess patent rights and potentially comply with the spirit of patent law regarding notice and disclosure. If invention details are vague, that can undermine the ability to determine if the invention is new, useful, and non-obvious.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 290 - Notice of patent suits | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 290 - Notice of patent suits U.S. Code Notes prev | next The clerks of the courts of the United States, within one month after the filing of an action under this title shall give notice thereof in writing to the Director, setting forth so far as known the names and addresses of the parties, name of the inventor, and the designating number of the patent upon which the action has been brought. If any other patent is subsequently included in the action he shall give like notice thereof. Within one month after the decision is rendered or a judgment issued the clerk of the court shall give notice thereof to the Director. The Director shall, on receipt of such notices, enter the same in the file of such patent. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 814 ; Pub. L. 106–113, div. perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'shall promptly provide' and 'results directly,' which create a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by using 'should endeavor to provide' and 'seems to result,' introducing ambiguity. The law explanation notes that weakening the requirement for prompt and clear disclosure hinders the ability to assess patent rights. While the provided snippets from 35 U.S. Code § 290 focus on notification of patent suits, the underlying principle is timely communication. The change from 'shall' to 'should endeavor' undermines the directness and certainty of the disclosure, potentially leading to delayed or incomplete disclosures, which goes against the need for clear communication in patent-related matters. This ambiguity creates a legally problematic situation where a party could avoid disclosing relevant inventions, making the agreement difficult to enforce and creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 8.4 CC-Pharming represents and warranties that it shall maintain all of iBio's Technology and technical information, how-how, documents, materials, software, vectors, constructs, trade secrets, and other valuable business or scientific information strictly confidential at all times, and shall take all steps necessary to safe-guard iBio's Technology and technical information with reasonable business care and will be of the same types as currently practiced by iBio to maintain its highly confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.4 CC-Pharming represents and warrants that it will attempt to maintain iBio's Technology and technical information, documents, materials, software, vectors, constructs, trade secrets, and other business information confidential, and shall take steps to safeguard such information with care.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause creates a strong obligation for CC-Pharming to maintain confidentiality. The modified version weakens this by replacing 'shall maintain...strictly confidential at all times' with 'will attempt to maintain...confidential' and removing 'how-how' and 'valuable or scientific information,' implying that not all information needs to be kept confidential. Removing the 'same types as currently practiced by iBio' removes a clear standard for safeguarding.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 addresses the theft of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to maintain strict confidentiality and removing explicit references to 'valuable or scientific information,' the modified clause creates a loophole that could allow CC-Pharming to argue that certain information was not considered a trade secret and thus not subject to the highest level of protection. The ambiguity introduced could make it more difficult to prove a violation of trade secret laws if CC-Pharming mishandles or discloses iBio's sensitive information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret , that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret , knowingly— (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains such information; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys such information; (3) receives, buys, or possesses such information, knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than 10 years, or both. (b) Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $5,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104–294, title I, § 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3489 ; amended Pub. L. 112–236, § 2 , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1627 ; Pub. L. 114–153, § 3(a)(1) , May 11, 2016 , 130 Stat. 382
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract's confidentiality obligations, potentially conflicting with trade secret protection laws. The original text mandates 'strictly confidential at all times' and 'all steps necessary to safe-guard iBio's Technology,' establishing a strong obligation. The changed text replaces this with 'attempt to maintain...confidential' and 'safeguard such information with care,' reducing the commitment to a weaker, effort-based standard. The law snippets (18 U.S. Code § 1832) address the theft of trade secrets. By diminishing the requirement for strict confidentiality, the revised contract introduces ambiguity. This could allow CC-Pharming to argue that specific information was not considered a trade secret and therefore not subject to stringent protection, making it more challenging to prove a violation of trade secret laws if CC-Pharming mishandles iBio's sensitive information. Therefore, the modified text creates a loophole that could be exploited.

perturbation.original_text: 9.13 Governing Law. This Agreement is governed by the laws of the State of Texas, without regard to the conflict of laws provisions thereof. Any proceedings to resolve disputes relating to this Agreement shall be brought only in the State of Texas and in a U.S. federal court if there is jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.13 Governing Law. This Agreement is generally intended to be interpreted under the laws of the State of Texas, considering relevant conflicts of law. Any proceedings to resolve disputes relating to this Agreement may be brought in Texas.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly establishes Texas law as the governing law and specifies the venue for dispute resolution. The modified version introduces ambiguity by using 'generally intended to be interpreted' instead of 'is governed,' suggesting flexibility in the applicable law. 'Considering relevant conflicts of law' adds further uncertainty. Replacing 'shall be brought only' with 'may be brought' weakens the venue requirement, potentially allowing disputes to be litigated elsewhere.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Civil Practice and Remedies Code § 15.002
perturbation.law_citation: Texas Civil Practice and Remedies Code § 15.002
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Civil Practice and Remedies Code § 15.002 allows parties to agree on a specific venue. By changing the mandatory 'shall be brought only in the State of Texas' to the permissive 'may be brought in Texas', the modified clause undermines the initial intent and enforceability of the forum selection clause, potentially conflicting with the certainty intended by the Texas statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE CHAPTER 15. VENUE CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE TITLE 2. TRIAL, JUDGMENT, AND APPEAL SUBTITLE B. TRIAL MATTERS CHAPTER 15. VENUE SUBCHAPTER A. DEFINITIONS; GENERAL RULES Sec. 15.001. DEFINITIONS. In this chapter: (a) "Principal office" means a principal office of the corporation, unincorporated association, or partnership in this state in which the decision makers for the organization within this state conduct the daily affairs of the organization. The mere presence of an agency or representative does not establish a principal office. (b) "Proper venue" means: (1) the venue required by the mandatory provisions of Subchapter B or another statute prescribing mandatory venue; or (2) if Subdivision (1) does not apply, the venue provided by this subchapter or Subchapter C. Added by Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 138, Sec. 1, eff. Aug. 28, 1995. Sec. 15.002. VENUE: GENERAL RULE. (a) Except as otherwise provided by this subchapter or Subchapter B or C, all lawsuits shall be brought: (1) in the county in which all or a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred; (2) in the county of defendant's residence at the time the cause of action accrued if defendant is a natural person; (3) in the county of the defendant's principal office in this state, if the defendant is not a natural person; or (4) if Subdivisions (1), (2), and (3) do not apply, in the county in which the plaintiff resided at the time of the accrual of the cause of action. (b) For the convenience of the parties and witnesses and in the interest of justice, a court may transfer an action from a county of proper venue under this subchapter or Subchapter C to any other county of proper venue on motion of a defendant filed and served concurrently with or before the filing of the answer, where the court finds: (1) maintenance of the action in the county of suit would work an injustice to the movant considering the movant's economic and personal hardship; (2) the balance of interests of all the parties predominates in favor of the action being brought in the other county; and (3) the transfer of the action would not work an injustice to any other party. (c) A court's ruling or decision to grant or deny a transfer under Subsection (b) is not grounds for appeal or mandamus and is not reversible error. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. Renumbered from Civil Practice & Remedies Code Sec. 15.001 and amended by Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 138, Sec. 1, eff. Aug. 28, 1995. The following section was amended by the 89th Legislature. Pending publication of the current statutes, see H.B. 40 , 89th Legislature, Regular Session, for amendments affecting the following section. Sec. 15.003. MULTIPLE PLAINTIFFS AND INTERVENING PLAINTIFFS. (a) In a suit in which there is more than one plaintiff, whether the plaintiffs are inc
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be brought only' to 'may be brought' directly weakens the forum selection clause. While Texas Civil Practice and Remedies Code § 15.002 doesn't explicitly mandate forum selection clauses, the law explanation points out that this statute allows parties to agree on a specific venue. The original text established a mandatory venue, and the modified text introduces ambiguity, making the enforceability of the venue agreement uncertain, thus contradicting the intent of the original agreement and creating a potential conflict with the certainty implied by the statute regarding venue agreements.
